title: Mildew on Cotton
stainChemistry: Mildew is a living fungal colony — not just a stain, an organism. Hyphae (root-like fungal threads) penetrate into cotton fibers; spores and metabolic pigments produce the visible gray/green/black discoloration. Left untreated, mildew keeps digesting the cellulose and the underlying cotton weakens. Cotton is durable: tolerates oxygen-side oxidizers (3% or 6% H₂O₂), chlorine bleach on whites, UV sunlight, and hot wash — all of which are active against mildew. The clean-up has two jobs: kill the live organism AND remove the pigment. Skip either half and it either grows back or leaves a visible shadow.
whyThisWorks: Oxygen-side chemistry does both jobs at once — 3% H₂O₂ or oxygen bleach (OxiClean sodium percarbonate in warm water releases H₂O₂) kills the organism AND oxidizes the pigment. Chlorine bleach kills faster but only works safely on whites. Sunlight + UV is a third kill-and-fade mechanism that doesn't cost anything. Hot wash after chemistry kills any remaining spores. Prevention is the fourth step and it's the one most home users skip: dry the garment COMPLETELY before storing, or mildew returns.
defaultAssumption: Treat mildew as both an organism (must be killed) and a stain (must be cleared). Assume the garment has been in a humid location (damp laundry pile, basement, mildewed closet) and that the root cause isn't fixed until the storage environment changes.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Stiff bristle brush, dust mask, well-ventilated area (outdoors preferred)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Brush dried surface mildew off outdoors or in a very well-ventilated space, wearing a dust mask. Mildew spores are respiratory irritants and you do not want to redistribute them through indoor air. Brush in one direction (away from you) over a drop cloth. This removes the loose biomass before any wet work.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ in a spray bottle or applied via clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Saturate the stained area with 3% H₂O₂. Peroxide is active against the fungal colony — it kills live hyphae and spores AND oxidizes the metabolic pigment. On whites the dose can step up to 6% if 3% doesn't clear the shadow. Dwell 10–15 minutes. Cotton tolerates peroxide at these concentrations without fiber damage.
Work NSD into the spot with a soft bristle brush. NSD lifts the now-dead fungal biomass and any oxidized pigment residue. Flush cool from the back of the fabric.

spottingProtocol.technique: Launder hot, dry in direct sunlight
spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, outdoor line or sunny window
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Full wash cycle, then 2–6 hours in sun
spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder hot per the care label. Heat kills any remaining fungal matter the peroxide missed. Dry outdoors in direct sunlight whenever possible — UV is a third antimicrobial mechanism and is the cleanest odor finisher for mildew. Do not store the garment until it is absolutely bone-dry; residual moisture is how mildew returns.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Bright light, the garment, the storage location
spottingProtocol.instruction: Inspect the garment in bright daylight for any lingering shadow. If present, repeat the peroxide + sun cycle. Then — critical — address WHERE the mildew came from: a damp laundry pile, a basement, a wet closet, a forgotten gym bag. Mildew on cotton isn't really solved until the garment's storage environment is dry. Otherwise the same fabric gets recolonized in days.

steps: 1. Brush off outdoors, mask on. Drop cloth beneath.
2. 3% H₂O₂ (6% on whites). Saturate, dwell 10–15 min.
3. NSD with warm water. Soft brush. Cool flush.
4. Hot wash per care label. Sun-dry outdoors when possible.
5. Identify and fix the moisture source — mildew returns otherwise.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient for spotting; hot wash post-chemistry; sun during dry
warnings: Always brush off dry mildew outdoors or in heavy ventilation — spores are respiratory irritants
6% peroxide on whites only — 3% is the default for anything dyed
One oxidizer at a time — never pair with chlorine bleach or ammonia
The stain doesn't stay fixed unless the storage moisture problem is fixed

homeSolutions.agent: Brush It Off Outdoors — Wear a Mask
homeSolutions.instruction: Take the garment outside (or to a well-ventilated porch / garage). Put on a dust mask — mildew spores are respiratory irritants, especially for people with allergies or asthma. Brush the surface mildew off with a stiff brush, brushing in one direction over a drop cloth or newspaper. Don't do this indoors if you can avoid it; the spores go airborne.

Mix one scoop of OxiClean in one gallon of WARM water (not hot — warm water activates the oxygen-bleach reaction; hot water breaks it down). Submerge the garment and let it soak 30 minutes to 2 hours. OxiClean kills the remaining mildew AND oxidizes the pigment.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for Dead Biomass
homeSolutions.instruction: After the soak, work a drop of Dawn dish soap into any remaining spots with a soft toothbrush. Dawn lifts the dead fungal material that the oxygen bleach killed. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.

If a gray or black shadow remains, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Let it sit 10 minutes, blot. Rinse cool. Skip this step on dark or dyed cotton — peroxide can fade the fabric color as well as the mildew.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean, hydrogen peroxide, and chlorine bleach — layering oxidizers produces toxic fumes. One oxidizer at a time.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Dry outside in direct sunlight if at all possible — UV kills any spores that survived. An hour or two in full sun is enough; longer is better for deeply colonized fabric. Bring it inside bone-dry, not damp.

homeSolutions.agent: Fix the Source — Otherwise It Comes Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Mildew doesn't grow in the fabric out of nowhere. Find out where the moisture came from — wet laundry left piled, a damp basement, a leaky gym bag, a humid closet — and address it. Dry storage is the only real prevention. Wash every fabric that shared the same storage spot. This step is the one most home treatments skip, and it's why mildew comes back.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate When the Fabric Is Compromised
homeSolutions.instruction: If the mildew has eaten through the weave (you can see thin or damaged spots), if the stain survives two full OxiClean + sun cycles, or if the item is a valuable/delicate fabric, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Mildew on cotton, [surface / penetrated]. Home-treated with OxiClean + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide + sun."

steps: 1. Brush off outdoors with a mask on. Drop cloth under.
2. OxiClean soak in WARM (not hot) water — 30 min to 2 hr.
3. Dawn dish soap + soft toothbrush on remaining spots. Cool rinse.
4. 3% hydrogen peroxide on stubborn shadows. Skip on dark cotton.
5. Hot wash per care label. Dry outside in direct sunlight.
6. Fix the storage / moisture source — otherwise mildew returns.
7. Escalate if fabric is damaged or two full cycles haven't cleared it.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the fabric shows weave damage (mildew ate through), if two full OxiClean + sun cycles don't clear the shadow, or if the item is delicate / valuable / dyed in a way that restricts the oxidizer lane.
warnings: Always brush off mildew outdoors with a mask — spores are respiratory irritants
Warm water activates OxiClean; hot water breaks it down — use warm not hot
Never layer OxiClean + peroxide + chlorine bleach — toxic fumes
Mildew returns if the storage moisture problem isn't fixed

neverDo: Never brush dry mildew in a closed room — spores are respiratory irritants
Never skip fixing the moisture source — the stain returns in days otherwise
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never store the garment damp — the mildew comes back overnight
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates oxygen bleach, 3–6% H₂O₂, chlorine bleach on whites, and hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Weakened cotton (if mildew has damaged the weave): decolorization possible but fiber integrity is lost — replace rather than reuse

materialWarnings: Brush off outdoors only — spores are respiratory irritants
Warm water for OxiClean, not hot
Fix the storage environment or the mildew returns
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Mildew has damaged the weave (visible thinning / holes), two OxiClean + sun cycles haven't cleared the shadow, or the item is delicate / valuable / dye-restricted.
escalation.whatToTell: "Mildew on cotton, [surface-only / penetrated fabric], home-treated with OxiClean + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide + sunlight. [Fabric integrity intact / weave damaged.]"

contextQuestions: Where was the garment stored — damp closet, basement, laundry pile, gym bag?
How long has the mildew been there — days, weeks, months?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Is the weave still strong, or does the fabric look thin in spots?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Mildew is an organism first, stain second. Killing it is step one; removing the pigment is step two; fixing the storage moisture is step three, and it's the one that decides whether the treatment actually holds.
